perturbation.original_text: Distributor shall provide to Vendor: (1) monthly sales figures no later than fifteen (15) calendar days after the end of the relevant monthly period that provides information with respect to sales for each Product; and (2) a non binding three (3) month forecast on a quarterly basis;
perturbation.changed_text: Distributor shall provide to Vendor: (1) monthly sales figures no later than thirty (30) calendar days after the end of the relevant monthly period that provides information with respect to sales for each Product; and (2) a non binding three (3) month forecast on an annual basis;
perturbation.explanation: Changing the forecast frequency from quarterly to annually reduces the information available to the vendor, potentially impacting their ability to manage inventory and production. This could violate the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing, which requires parties to act honestly and reasonably in the performance of a contract. It also impacts business predictability, which the original text promotes.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Implied Duty of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_explanation: The implied duty of good faith requires both parties to act reasonably and honestly. Reducing the frequency of sales forecasts from quarterly to annually hinders the Vendor's ability to manage its business effectively, potentially undermining the purpose of the distribution agreement and breaching the implied duty of good faith.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the frequency of sales forecasts from quarterly to annually, which can hinder the Vendor's ability to manage their business effectively. This violates the implied duty of good faith, according to both the perturbation and law explanations. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states, "Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement." By reducing the frequency of forecasts, the Distributor potentially fails to act in good faith, creating a contradiction. The change directly impacts the Vendor's ability to manage inventory and production, and reduces business predictability. The change is explicit and impactful, fitting the criteria for a 'YES' determination.

perturbation.original_text: For any price change to take effect ninety (90) days notice must be given in order for the price change to apply; provided, however, the proposed price change must also apply to the Products for sale by Vendor outside the Territory.
perturbation.changed_text: For any price change to take effect thirty (30) days notice must be given in order for the price change to apply; provided, however, the proposed price change must also apply to the Products for sale by Vendor outside the Territory.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the price change notice period from 90 days to 30 days may violate the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing. While the original text provides the distributor with ample time to adjust its sales strategy, the changed text significantly reduces this time, which could negatively affect the distributor's profitability and ability to fulfill its obligations.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Implied Duty of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_explanation: Reducing the notice period for price changes could be seen as a breach of the implied duty of good faith. The shorter notice period gives the Distributor less time to react and adjust its business, potentially causing harm and acting contrary to the spirit of a fair agreement.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The original contract provides 90 days' notice for price changes, while the changed text reduces this to 30 days. This inconsistency can create a legal contradiction if the reduced notice period violates the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both suggest that a shorter notice period could harm the distributor's ability to adjust its business, thereby contradicting the spirit of a fair agreement. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states that "Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement." Therefore, reducing the notice period could be seen as undermining this obligation, leading to a potential legal contradiction, as described in the Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction definition. The change introduces a time-sensitive obligation that may not align with the legal requirement of good faith.

perturbation.original_text: Distributor may return Products to Vendor for credit or full reimbursement, as the case may be, only pursuant to this Section 8. 8.2 Updated Products. Distributor may return to Vendor for credit against future purchases any Products for which a new version or upgrade has been produced and offered for sale; provided, however, the new version or upgrade must be of a material nature whereby the existing Products held by Distributor are considered obsolete. All Products must be returned undamaged, and all shipping charges -7- shall be paid by Distributor. This clause does not apply to any Products that have been in Distributor's inventory for more that one hundred and eighty (180) calendar days.
perturbation.changed_text: Distributor may return Products to Vendor for credit or full reimbursement, as the case may be, only pursuant to this Section 8. 8.2 Updated Products. Distributor may return to Vendor for credit against future purchases any Products for which a new version or upgrade has been produced and offered for sale; provided, however, the new version or upgrade must be of a material nature whereby the existing Products held by Distributor are considered obsolete. All Products must be returned undamaged, and all shipping charges -7- shall be paid by Distributor. This clause does not apply to any Products that have been in Distributor's inventory for more that thirty (30) calendar days.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the period for returning outdated products from 180 days to 30 days is unreasonable, and may violate the implied duty of good faith. It limits the distributor's ability to manage its inventory and reduces its ability to return products that have become obsolete due to newer versions being released. This could be seen as an attempt to unfairly burden the distributor with outdated products.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Implied Duty of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_explanation: By severely restricting the return window for updated products, the altered text violates the implicit understanding of fair dealing within a business contract. It puts an undue strain on the distributor, potentially harming their business and contradicting good faith business practices.

perturbation.justification: The changed text, by reducing the return window from 180 to 30 days, potentially violates the implied duty of good faith, as described in the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states: 'Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement.' The reduction seems unreasonable and could unduly burden the distributor. While Snippet 2 is missing, Snippet 1 sufficiently highlights the legal basis of good faith, which the modification appears to undermine, creating a contradiction. The 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction' definition applies because the modification creates a time-sensitive obligation that may violate state law related to good faith.
